w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Authored by: Founding CTO / Principal Architect
Classification: WORKER CLAUDE EXECUTION PACKAGE — ONE-TIME TRANSFER

: This document IS the product. Every architectural decision, every implementation order, every prompt, every risk — it's all here. Do not ask for clarification. Build exactly this. When in doubt, re-read this doc.
— Surfshark-style GUI with PQC handshake engine, multi-mode tunnel selector, and real-time integrity monitor.
— GCP-hosted tunnel exit node + peer relay coordinator.
The same binary runs in three modes:
. Mode is set at runtime via config.
Privacy-first individuals who understand quantum threat timelines.
Gamers who need low-latency tunneling with optional security downgrade.
Peer-relay users (two friends tunneling through each other's internet connection).
Developer/builder use case: proof of PQC networking capability.
Standard VPNs use X25519 + ChaCha20. A quantum computer running Shor's algorithm breaks X25519.
PQVPN replaces the key exchange with ML-KEM-768 (NIST FIPS 203) in a hybrid X25519+Kyber mode
, giving harvest-now-decrypt-later protection today, with zero performance penalty on modern hardware.
Windows client ↔ GCP server tunnel (Mode 1: Server Mode)
Surfshark-inspired UI: connect button, connection state, PQC badge
Basic integrity monitor (handshake age, packet stats)
Peer relay mode (Mode 2: Friend's PC as exit node)
Gaming mode (Mode 3: PQC off, WireGuard native, minimum latency)
Mobile clients (iOS/Android via Tauri mobile or React Native)
Decentralized peer mesh (no central server required)
Paid tier: managed server nodes in 20+ regions
Client connects to GCP server via PQC-augmented WireGuard tunnel
All traffic exits through GCP's IP
PQC handshake active: X25519 + ML-KEM-768 hybrid KEM
Integrity monitor running: shows handshake age, PQC key status, packet loss
Client A connects to GCP server for signaling
Server coordinates with Client B (who accepted relay role)
NAT traversal attempted (UDP hole-punching via STUN)
Client B must be running PQVPN in "Relay Accept" mode
Invite link system: Client B shares a link → Client A joins relay
Uses native WireGuard Noise_IKpsk2 protocol (X25519 + ChaCha20-Poly1305)
Typical WireGuard latency: ~1-3ms overhead vs 8-15ms for PQC handshake
Security warning shown in UI: "Gaming Mode Active — PQC Disabled"
Connection integrity monitor still runs (without PQC metrics)
Tamper detection status (hash verification of tunnel params)
— Enter friend's invite code OR generate your own invite link
— Server region, auto-reconnect, kill switch, DNS leak protection
Peer registry (who is connected, what mode)
Signaling server for P2P relay coordination
: As a user, I open PQVPN, click Connect in Server Mode, and my traffic routes through GCP with PQC-secured tunnel within 3 seconds.
: As a user, I want to see a green "PQC Active" badge confirming my handshake used ML-KEM-768.
: As a user, I share my invite link to a friend. They accept relay mode. I switch to Peer Relay and my traffic exits through their internet.
: As a gamer, I tap "Gaming Mode", PQC disables with a warning, latency drops by ~10ms, I get better ping.
: As a user, if the tunnel drops, Kill Switch blocks all internet until reconnected.
: As a user, the Integrity Monitor shows me if my tunnel has been idle (no handshake) or if something looks wrong.
: fall back to GCP relay for peer mode
: refuse to connect in standard mode (show hard error), allow gaming mode only
: graceful disconnect, offer fallback to server mode
: show error state, no silent degradation
: transparent to user, logged in integrity monitor
: WireGuard handshake fails if clock difference > 180s. Client must sync NTP before connecting.
: must persist. Store kill switch flag in Windows registry.
PQC handshake timeout (>5s): retry 3x, then fall back with user confirmation
Tunnel MTU mismatch: auto-detect and set correct MTU (1420 for most, 1280 for some ISPs)
DNS leak through Windows default resolver: force all DNS through tunnel or use DoH/DoT
Tunnel throughput: ≥ 500 Mbps (limited by GCP egress tier, not crypto)
│ │ Tauri UI │ │ PQC Engine │ │ WireGuard Driver │ │
│ │ - Kill Switch (Windows firewall rules via netsh/WFP) │ │
│ Same PQVPN binary in "Relay Accept" mode │
│ Routes Client A's traffic through Client B's internet │
The WireGuard Noise protocol uses X25519 for its DH key exchange. We
this with a hybrid KEM pre-exchange:
Initiator → Responder: ephemeral X25519 pubkey + encrypted static pubkey
Responder → Initiator: ephemeral X25519 pubkey + encrypted static pubkey
Both sides derive: psk = KDF(kyber_ss || x25519_ss)
Normal WireGuard Noise_IKpsk2 using psk from above
This authenticates AND encrypts the data channel
Result: Even if X25519 is broken by quantum computer, kyber_ss protects the PSK.
Data channel: ChaCha20-Poly1305 (unchanged from WireGuard standard).
│ └── commands.rs # Tauri IPC commands exposed to frontend
│ │ ├── server.go # WebSocket signaling for peer relay
│ │ ├── stun.go # STUN server for NAT traversal
Client registers with server: POST /api/v1/register {wg_pubkey, pqc_pubkey, device_id}
Server stores peer, returns server's WG pubkey + PQC pubkey + JWT
Client sends JWT in PQC ClientHello (signed with ML-DSA-65 identity key)
Client B generates invite token = HMAC-SHA256(peerB_identity, timestamp, nonce)
Client A receives token via out-of-band (share link, QR code)
Client A sends token to server during relay setup
Server validates token, signals Client B
config.toml — user preferences, cached server endpoint
identity.key — ML-DSA-65 keypair (encrypted with user PIN or Windows DPAPI)
peers table — {id, wg_pubkey, pqc_pubkey, jwt_hash, created_at, last_seen}
relay_sessions table — {id, peer_a_id, peer_b_id, token_hash, state, created_at}
Client A GCP Server Client B
GCP Region: asia-south1 (Mumbai) — lowest latency from Chennai
Static external IP: required (configure in GCP console)
Domain: Optional but recommended for production (use GCP Cloud DNS)
Rust backend, small binary (~8MB), native OS APIs
WireGuard ecosystem is Go-native, liboqs-go exists
Low complexity, works through most firewalls
Slower iteration than Go for network services
6 months more work, worse security audit baseline
OpenVPN is legacy, UDP performance poor
GCP has better free tier + lower Asia-Pacific latency
Overkill, adds ops burden for MVP
Do NOT build your own crypto primitives.
Do NOT try to fork WireGuard source.
Augment it with the PQC pre-exchange layer. WireGuard itself is untouched.
Do NOT use a pre-shared key UI
where users manually enter PSKs. It breaks UX.
Do NOT use TCP for the tunnel.
TCP-over-TCP is catastrophically bad for VPN performance.
Do NOT store private keys unencrypted.
Do NOT build a mobile client for MVP.
Replace SQLite with CockroachDB when scaling to 10k+ peers
Add Noise_PQXeneq when IETF standardizes it (2025-2026 timeline)
Tauri mobile when stable (iOS/Android same codebase)
P0-001: PQC Library Windows Build Fails
crate requires liboqs C library. Cross-compilation on Windows is painful.
Mitigation: Use pre-built liboqs DLL for Windows. Bundle in Tauri sidecar. Test this on Day 1.
If it fails: Fall back to pure-Rust
P0-002: WireGuard Driver Requires Admin Privileges
Risk: wintun.dll requires admin to create network interface on Windows
Mitigation: Use Windows service + UAC elevation prompt on first install. This is expected for VPN software.
Tauri supports Windows service installers via NSIS/WiX.
Risk: GCP default firewall blocks all UDP
Mitigation: Add explicit firewall rule in GCP console during server setup. Document in SETUP.md.
Risk: UDP hole-punching fails for users behind symmetric NAT (~20-30% of consumer routers)
Mitigation: DERP relay fallback through GCP server. Always works, slightly higher latency.
Accept this: relay fallback is not degradation, it's design.
Risk: ML-KEM-768 encapsulation + ML-DSA-65 verification adds measurable latency on first connect
Numbers: Kyber-768 keygen ~50μs, encaps ~70μs, verify ~120μs on modern CPU. Not the bottleneck.
Real bottleneck: network RTT for the extra round-trip. Mitigate: pipeline PQC pre-exchange with first WG packet.
Risk: WireGuard re-handshakes every 180 seconds by default. Our PQC pre-exchange must also re-run.
Mitigation: Implement PQC PSK rotation hook that fires on WireGuard re-handshake signal. Cache Kyber keys for 180s window.
Risk: If PQVPN crashes while kill switch is active, user has no internet.
Mitigation: Kill switch writes firewall rules to Windows Registry. Recovery: PQVPN auto-launches on system start. Manual override in tray: "Emergency disable kill switch".
Standard Windows DNS resolver ignores tunnel routing for some apps
Mitigation: Force DNS to 1.1.1.1 or 8.8.8.8 THROUGH tunnel. Set WireGuard DNS field. Add Windows NRPT policy.
WireGuard default MTU 1420. Some ISPs/networks need 1280.
Mitigation: Auto-detect via PMTUD. Expose manual override in settings.
Friend's PC can see your traffic (plaintext after exit)
Mitigation: UI warning: "Your traffic exits through your peer's internet. Trust your peer." Non-optional disclaimer.
One server goes down, all users lose connectivity
Mitigation: Not MVP concern. V2: multi-region, health-check-based failover.
— Ship dark mode first, light mode later
— Tauri has built-in updater, wire it up in V1
— Prometheus resolution limited by polling interval
[ ] GCP VM provisioned (Ubuntu 22.04, static IP, firewall rules configured)
[ ] liboqs compiled on server (Ubuntu build is straightforward)
[ ] Tauri v2 dev environment on Windows (Rust toolchain + Node 20)
[ ] oqs-rs crate verified building on Windows (THIS IS YOUR FIRST TEST)
[ ] GitHub repo created with monorepo structure (see Section 7)
Windows build. If this fails on Day 1, switch to
crate immediately. Do not spend more than 4 hours on it.
[ ] Implement PQC pre-exchange protocol (client-side TCP/TLS socket)
[ ] Mirror on Go server: liboqs-go KEM + DSA
[ ] Integration test: client ↔ server PQC handshake, both derive identical PSK
[ ] wintun interface creation on Windows (requires admin, test in dev first)
CLI automation in Go (add peer, remove peer)
[ ] End-to-end: client ↔ server tunnel with PQC PSK, ping test passes
[ ] Tauri project scaffold with React 18 + TypeScript + Tailwind
[ ] Design system: colors (#0A0A0F dark bg, #00D4FF cyan accent, #FF4757 red for disconnected)
[ ] Main screen: giant connect button, status ring animation, PQC badge
[ ] Mode selector: Server / Peer Relay / Gaming tabs
[ ] Integrity Monitor panel (expandable, shows real-time metrics)
[ ] System tray icon with status colors
[ ] Tauri IPC: frontend ↔ Rust backend command bindings
[ ] Settings screen: server endpoint, kill switch toggle, DNS settings
Phase 3: Server Infrastructure, parallel with UI)
[ ] WireGuard peer management: add/remove peers via
[ ] DERP relay fallback (route relay mode traffic through GCP when P2P fails)
[ ] Client: relay session state machine (PENDING → SIGNALING → CONNECTING → ACTIVE → CLOSED)
[ ] UI: "Generate invite link" button, QR code display, "Enter invite code" input
[ ] End-to-end peer relay test: Client A through Client B's internet
[ ] Mode 3: disable PQC pre-exchange entirely, connect with standard WireGuard PSK
[ ] Latency comparison UI element: shows current ping, ping delta vs PQC mode
[ ] Security warning modal on gaming mode activation (non-dismissible first time)
[ ] Persist gaming mode preference (user opted in explicitly)
[ ] DNS leak prevention (NRPT policy via PowerShell, called from Rust)
[ ] DPAPI key encryption for stored private keys
[ ] Unit tests: PQC keygen + exchange (both sides must derive same PSK)
[ ] Integration test: full tunnel up/down cycle automated
[ ] Peer relay E2E test with two VMs
[ ] Kill switch test: verify no traffic leaks when tunnel drops
[ ] Gaming mode latency benchmark: WireGuard-only vs PQC mode
main — production-ready only. Protected. Requires PR + review.
develop — integration branch. CI must pass.
fix(client): resolve wintun interface not releasing on disconnect
docs(adr): add ADR-003 for PQC algorithm choice
test(server): add integration test for peer registry
chore(infra): add GCP firewall setup script
Describe: what, why, how to test
Worker Claude should be given one task at a time using the prompts in Section 10.
Use Claude Opus model for architecture review prompts
Use Claude Sonnet (or the 120B OSS model) for implementation
: Open prompt → paste context from this doc + current file → implement → commit
Keep context window focused: only pass the relevant module files + the relevant section of this doc
Use Snitch for the React frontend UI implementation
Paste the UI spec from Section 2 (Screens section) into Snitch
Reference: Surfshark app screenshots as visual direction
Snitch workflow: design → export to React components → integrate into Tauri
Antigravity: can read/write files, commit, push
Worker Claude: generates code blocks → you copy-paste OR use Antigravity direct commit
PR review: use Claude to review PRs (
Issue tracking: create GitHub Issues for each task in TASK_GRAPH.md
: Claude can write the full Rust PQC module in one shot with the right prompt (see Prompt 3 below)
: Claude generates entire Go server skeleton in <5 min
: Snitch generates Surfshark-style React components from description
: Claude writes the full setup.sh in one shot
: Claude writes integration tests from protocol spec
A post-quantum secured VPN with three modes: Server, Peer Relay, and Gaming.
(This entire Section 3 above is the content of this file)
- [ ] T005: Tauri dev env on Windows (CRITICAL PATH)
- [ ] T006: oqs-rs Windows build test (CRITICAL PATH — test Day 1)
- [ ] T012: Hybrid PSK derivation (depends: T010, T011)
- [ ] T014: Go server PQC mirror (depends: T013)
- [ ] T015: WireGuard config gen Rust (depends: T012)
- [ ] T017: Server WireGuard peer management (depends: T014)
- [ ] T018: E2E tunnel test with PQC (depends: T016, T017) ← MILESTONE 1
Goal: GCP running, Windows client can complete PQC handshake with server
Done when: `ping 10.0.0.1` succeeds through PQC-negotiated WireGuard tunnel
Goal: Surfshark-style UI connected to real backend, Mode 1 fully functional
Done when: User can connect/disconnect via GUI, PQC badge shows correctly
Goal: Two Windows machines can route through each other
Done when: Client A surfs internet through Client B's connection
Goal: All 3 modes working, kill switch, DNS leak protection
Done when: All E2E tests pass
Goal: Installable .exe, auto-update, all edge cases handled
Done when: Fresh Windows 10 VM can install and connect in <2 min
(This is Section 5 of this document, copied verbatim)
Build PQVPN exactly as specified in MASTER_HANDOFF.md.
- oqs-rs (Rust PQC client) + liboqs-go (Go PQC server)
1. Run: `cargo add oqs` in a test project. If it compiles, proceed. If not, use `pqcrypto` instead.
2. Create the GitHub repo with the folder structure in MASTER_HANDOFF.md Section 7.
3. Begin with EPIC-0 (infrastructure) then EPIC-1 (PQC handshake).
- Never skip tests for PQC crypto. A wrong PSK = silent tunnel break.
- Always test with two actual network interfaces, not loopback.
- Commit working state at end of every task.
- If blocked >2 hours, document the blocker and move to next task.
- Feature tested end-to-end on real hardware
You are implementing PQVPN, a post-quantum secured VPN.
Create the complete GitHub repository structure for a Rust/Tauri v2 client + Go server monorepo.
├── client/ (Tauri v2 project: React 18 + TypeScript + Tailwind)
1. Run `npm create tauri-app@latest client` with React + TypeScript
2. Initialize Go module: `go mod init github.com/YOUR_ORG/pqvpn/server`
3. Create all directories and placeholder files
4. Create root README.md with project overview
Output: all files with full content, ready to commit as first commit.
You are implementing the GCP server setup for PQVPN.
- Updates apt, installs wireguard, golang-1.22, sqlite3, git, build-essential
- Compiles liboqs from source (github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs, cmake build)
- Server private key (generated on first run)
- PostUp/PostDown: iptables NAT rules for traffic forwarding
- Creates systemd service for pqvpnd (see file 3)
- Opens ports: UDP 51820, TCP 8443, TCP 8444, UDP 3478
GCP gcloud commands to open all required ports (UDP 51820, TCP 8443, TCP 8444, UDP 3478)
Systemd unit file for the PQVPN Go daemon
All scripts must be idempotent (safe to run multiple times).
Include error handling. Print status messages at each step.
PROMPT 3: BACKEND — PQC HANDSHAKE + WIREGUARD (RUST CLIENT)
You are implementing the PQC crypto and WireGuard management layer for PQVPN Windows client (Tauri v2, Rust backend).
- PQC Pre-Exchange (runs BEFORE WireGuard handshake, over TCP:8443 TLS)
- Client sends: ClientHello { ml_kem_pubkey: [u8;1184], x25519_pubkey: [u8;32], timestamp: u64, signature: [u8;3309] }
where signature = ML-DSA-65.sign(identity_key, ml_kem_pubkey || x25519_pubkey || timestamp)
- Server responds: ServerResponse { kem_ciphertext: [u8;1088], x25519_pubkey: [u8;32], signature: [u8;3309] }
- Both derive: psk = HKDF-SHA256(ikm = kyber_ss || x25519_dh_result, info = b"pqvpn-psk-v1", len = 32)
- WireGuard uses this psk as PreSharedKey in wg0.conf
3. src-tauri/src/pqc/handshake.rs - Full ClientHello send + response parse + PSK derivation
4. src-tauri/src/wireguard/config.rs - Generate wg0.conf with given PSK, server endpoint, client keypair
5. src-tauri/src/wireguard/driver.rs - Start/stop WireGuard via `wg-quick` subprocess (or wireguard-nt)
6. src-tauri/src/wireguard/manager.rs - High-level: connect(server_ip) → runs PQC → runs WireGuard
Include full unit tests for PQC: test that keygen + encaps/decaps produces matching shared secrets.
Include integration test: client and server sides derive identical PSK.
You are implementing the PQVPN Go server daemon.
Architecture: Go 1.22 service with HTTP API + WebSocket signaling + STUN server
- Handler: receives ClientHello, validates signature, encapsulates, derives PSK, returns ServerResponse
- Schema: peers(id TEXT PK, wg_pubkey TEXT, pqc_pubkey TEXT, jwt_hash TEXT, last_seen INTEGER)
- relay_sessions(id TEXT PK, peer_a TEXT, peer_b TEXT, token TEXT, state TEXT, created_at INTEGER)
- Functions: RegisterPeer, GetPeer, UpdateLastSeen, CreateRelaySession, GetRelaySession
- HTTP server on :8443 (TLS with self-signed cert for now)
POST /api/v1/connect → trigger PQC handshake + add WireGuard peer
- AddPeer(wg_pubkey, allowed_ip) — runs `wg set wg0 peer <pubkey> allowed-ips <ip>`
- RemovePeer(wg_pubkey) — runs `wg set wg0 peer <pubkey> remove`
Use only standard library + these specific deps:
Include full test coverage for registry and PQC handshake handler.
You are implementing the PQVPN Windows client UI in React 18 + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS.
- Dark theme only. Background: #080B12 (near-black with blue tint)
- Font: "Space Mono" for monospace elements, "Plus Jakarta Sans" for UI
- Style: Surfshark-inspired with cyberpunk/quantum tech aesthetic
- Animations: Framer Motion for all state transitions
- Center: Giant circular connect button (80px radius)
- Disconnected: gray ring, power icon, "CONNECT" label
- Connected: solid cyan ring, checkmark, "CONNECTED" label
- Below button: current IP address (masked when disconnected)
" in cyan when on, "GAMING MODE" in orange when off
- Bottom: mode selector tabs (SERVER | PEER RELAY | GAMING)
- SERVER: shield icon, "Route through our server"
- PEER RELAY: network icon, "Route through a friend"
- GAMING: lightning icon, "Low latency, PQC disabled" (orange warning)
- Switching modes while connected: show confirmation modal
- Collapsible panel (click to expand from bottom of screen)
- Shows: Last Handshake, PQC Key Age, Bytes In/Out, Packet Loss %, Tamper Status
- All values update every 5 seconds via Tauri IPC
- Color coding: green = healthy, yellow = warning, red = alert
- Join: text input for invite code + connect button
- Host: QR code display + copy-link button + toggle "Accept relay requests"
- Full-screen modal on first gaming mode activation
- Warning: "PQC encryption disabled. Your handshake is vulnerable to quantum attacks. Only use for gaming sessions."
Tauri IPC commands to call (already implemented in Rust backend):
- invoke('get_status') → { state, mode, pqc_active, uptime, bytes_in, bytes_out }
Use Zustand for global state (connection state, mode, metrics).
You are writing the test suite for PQVPN.
1. Rust Unit Tests (add to existing modules)
- test_ml_kem_768_roundtrip: keygen → encaps → decaps → assert shared secrets equal
- test_ml_dsa_65_sign_verify: keygen → sign message → verify → assert true
- test_psk_derivation: given fixed kyber_ss + x25519_dh, assert PSK is deterministic and 32 bytes
- test_hybrid_kem: full ClientHello generation + ServerResponse parsing + PSK derivation
- TestServerHandshakeFlow: generate client keys, call server handler, verify PSK matches
- TestSignatureVerification: invalid signature must be rejected
- TestRegisterPeer: register → get → assert all fields match
- TestRelaySession: create session → update state → verify transitions
- Spin up server in Docker (or locally)
- Assert: PQC handshake log line present
- Assert: WireGuard peer appears in `wg show`
- Teardown: disconnect, assert WireGuard peer removed
- Assert all DNS servers are tunnel-routed (no local ISP servers visible)
All tests must run in CI (GitHub Actions). Include the workflow additions needed.
You are writing the production deployment config for PQVPN server.
- Print: server IP, WireGuard pubkey, PQC public key
- Generate server ML-DSA-65 identity keypair (stored in /etc/pqvpn/)
- Trigger on: push to main with version tag (v*)
- Build: Tauri Windows installer (.exe) using tauri-action
- Upload: both as GitHub Release assets
- Use Let's Encrypt via certbot if domain available
- Fallback: self-signed cert (acceptable for MVP, document the browser warning)
- Grafana Cloud free tier: dashboard JSON with panels for:
Output all files with full content.
You are writing the PQVPN technical documentation.
Full specification of the PQVPN PQC pre-exchange protocol:
- Motivation: why X25519 alone is insufficient
- Why hybrid (X25519 + Kyber): defense in depth
- Wire format: exact byte layout of ClientHello and ServerResponse
- PSK derivation: HKDF inputs, output length, context string
- Gaming mode: what is disabled, security implications
- Threat model: what is protected, what is not
Step-by-step: GCP account setup → VM creation → setup.sh → verify → obtain server config
Step-by-step: Download installer → run → enter server config → connect
Architecture Decision Records for each major choice.
Format: Status, Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives Rejected
Add: architecture diagram (ASCII), quick demo GIF placeholder, security section, contributing guide
You are hardening PQVPN for real-world use.
Implement Windows Filtering Platform (WFP) based kill switch:
- Block all traffic when kill switch enabled EXCEPT WireGuard tunnel interface and loopback
- Persist state to Windows Registry: HKCU\Software\PQVPN\KillSwitchEnabled
- On app crash: kill switch state persists (by design — user must manually recover)
Use Windows NRPT (Name Resolution Policy Table) via PowerShell:
- When connected: route all DNS through tunnel (add NRPT rule forcing 1.1.1.1 via tunnel interface)
- Tauri command: set_dns_protection(enabled: bool, tunnel_interface: String)
- Background tokio task monitors tunnel state every 10 seconds
- If tunnel drops: exponential backoff reconnect (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, 30s max)
- Max retries: 10, then notify UI to show "reconnect failed" state
- Store ML-DSA-65 identity key encrypted via Windows DPAPI
- Use crate: windows-dpapi or encrypt via CryptProtectData win32 API
- Keys NEVER written to disk in plaintext
- Zeroize keys from memory after use (zeroize crate)
- Max 10 register attempts per IP per hour
- Max 100 connect attempts per peer per hour
Output all implementation files with full, working code.
Go server skeleton, Tauri setup, WireGuard config generation — these are all known patterns. Claude writes them in minutes. Do NOT hand-code these.
Leverage 2: liboqs does the hard crypto
You are NOT implementing ML-KEM. You are calling
. The hard part is the protocol design (done here) and the glue code.
Leverage 3: WireGuard does the hard networking
You are NOT writing a VPN protocol. You are augmenting WireGuard with a PQC pre-exchange. WireGuard's correctness is pre-established.
GCP setup + oqs-rs Windows build test (CRITICAL)
PQC handshake (both sides, all in Claude with Prompt 3+4)
Full Surfshark UI via Prompt 5 + Snitch
Go server daemon via Prompt 4 (if not done Week 1)
End: Mode 1 fully functional via UI
Focus on signaling server + client state machine
NAT traversal: STUN first, DERP fallback (don't over-engineer)
Gaming mode: 1 day (it's just disabling PQC)
Hardening: Kill switch + DNS + auto-reconnect (use Prompt 9)
Tauri installer build (GitHub Actions with release.yml)
Eliminations (things you don't need for V1)
macOS client (Windows first, macOS is same codebase later)

Build a post-quantum secured VPN for Windows with three operational modes: standard server exit (ML-KEM-768 hybrid), peer relay through a friend's machine, and gaming mode with PQC disabled for minimum latency. Ship in 35 days.
[ ] Nothing built yet. This document is the product spec. Start with EPIC-0.
Windows Tauri client ↔ PQC pre-exchange (ML-KEM-768 + X25519 hybrid) ↔ WireGuard tunnel ↔ GCP Go server ↔ Internet. Three client modes. One codebase.
compiles on Windows. If not → switch to
. This is the only unknown that can derail the stack.
on GCP VM. Verify WireGuard + liboqs installed.
Implement PQC handshake (Rust client + Go server) with Prompt 3+4.
Build Surfshark UI with Prompt 5 + Snitch.
FIRST THING WORKER CLAUDE SHOULD BUILD
Run Prompt 1 (Project Bootstrap) to create the full repository structure. Then immediately run:
cargo new pqc-test && cargo add oqs
and write 10 lines testing ML-KEM-768 keygen. This is the canary. Everything else depends on it working.
Exactly as specified in Section 7. Run Prompt 1. It outputs all files. Commit as:
chore: initial project scaffold — PQVPN monorepo
also full comit this is begining
git commit -m "chore: initial project scaffold — PQVPN monorepo
Stack: Rust/Tauri + React/TypeScript + Go + WireGuard + ML-KEM-768"

Worker Claude: you have everything. The architecture is decided. The stack is chosen. The risks are known. The prompts are ready. Build in order. Test after each EPIC. Do not deviate from the PQC protocol spec (Section 3). Do not replace the stack. When blocked, document and move forward. The most important thing you can do in the first 4 hours is verify that
builds on Windows and that a WireGuard tunnel works between your development machine and the GCP server.
Document version: 1.0 | Generated: 2026-05-19 | Author: Founding CTO
